channel.title: NYT > U.S. > Politics
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Why Were These C.E.O.s in Beijing With Trump?
item.description: Some of America’s most powerful C.E.O.s accompanied President Trump to Beijing during his summit with President Xi Jinping of China. Our reporter Ana Swanson explains what they were hoping to gain from the trip.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson, Nour Idriss, Nikolay Nikolov and James Surdam

item.title: Top Drug Regulator Is Fired From the F.D.A.
item.description: Dr. Tracy Beth Hoeg said she did not know who had fired her or why, but the dismissal came amid tumult at the agency in recent days.
item.dc:creator: Christina Jewett and Apoorva Mandavilli
item.media:credit: Anne Chadwick Williams for The New York Times
item.media:description: Dr. Tracy Beth Hoeg’s hiring had startled public health leaders familiar with her track record as a vaccine skeptic. But she defended her record at the Food and Drug Administration on Friday, saying she “stuck with the science.”

item.title: F.A.A. Says Better Scheduling Can Address Air Traffic Control Staffing Issues
item.description: In a new report, the F.A.A. says that by increasing controllers’ active work hours per shift, it could reduce its target for a fully staffed work force by over 2,000 positions.
item.dc:creator: Karoun Demirjian
item.media:credit: Bryan Anselm for The New York Times
item.media:description: The air traffic control tower at Newark Liberty International Airport in Newark, N.J. For years, controllers have been complaining of exhaustion, fatigue and low morale.

item.title: Supreme Court Rejects Virginia Democrats’ Effort to Reinstate New Voting Map
item.description: State officials had asked the justices to step in to allow the state to use a congressional map in the midterms that was drawn by Democrats and recently approved by voters.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Supreme Court’s ruling blocking the map wipes out four newly drawn Democratic-leaning House districts in Virginia.

item.title: Will the Iran War Backlash Save Rep. Thomas Massie in Kentucky?
item.description: A view from Thomas Massie’s district in Kentucky.
item.dc:creator: Katie Glueck
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Michael Swensen for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Thomas Massie of Kentucky

item.title: Ahead of U.S. 250th Anniversary, More National Guard Troops to Arrive in D.C.
item.description: Federal officials say that hundreds of additional agents, officers and National Guard troops will be deployed ahead of the nation’s 250th birthday celebration.
item.dc:creator: Campbell Robertson
item.category: US Federal Takeover of Metropolitan Police Dept (DC)
item.media:credit: Allison Robbert for The New York Times
item.media:description: Members of the Mississippi National Guard near the Washington Monument in April.

item.title: With Possible Raúl Castro Indictment, U.S. Eyes Venezuela Playbook
item.description: Amid stepped-up surveillance flights, a visit of the C.I.A. director and an energy embargo, the White House is trying to increase pressure on Cuba.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes, Tyler Pager, Eric Schmitt, Michael Crowley and Frances Robles
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Stephen Crowley/The New York Times
item.media:description: Raúl Castro is the former president of Cuba and younger brother of Fidel Castro.

item.title: Tina Peters, Colorado Election Denier, Will Be Freed by Gov. Jared Polis
item.description: Gov. Jared Polis, a Democrat, commuted the sentence of Ms. Peters, a former county clerk serving a nine-year sentence for her role in a plot to examine voting machines after the 2020 election.
item.dc:creator: Jack Healy and Nick Corasaniti
item.media:credit: Daniel Brenner for The New York Times
item.media:description: Tina Peters, the former clerk of Mesa County, Colo., was sentenced to nine years in prison in 2024 after being found guilty of tampering with voting machines.

item.title: Trump Administration Weighs $1.7 Billion Fund for Allies Investigated Under Biden
item.description: Critics denounced the highly unusual plan, which has yet to be finalized or approved, as a vast political slush fund financed by taxpayers.
item.dc:creator: Glenn Thrush, Andrew Duehren and Alan Feuer
item.media:credit: Stefani Reynolds for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump controls both the I.R.S., the agency he sued, and the Justice Department, which would typically represent the I.R.S. against legal claims.

item.title: Snorkeling at Pearl Harbor: Kash Patel’s Travels Add to Focus on Ethical Issues
item.description: The F.B.I. director continues to come under scrutiny for mixing government business with dates, vacation and leisure time.
item.dc:creator: Elizabeth Williamson and Adam Goldman
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Kyodo News, via Associated Press
item.media:description: The memorial above the sunken battleship U.S.S. Arizona in Hawaii in 2016. Kash Patel, the F.B.I. director, was accompanied by Navy SEALs on a “V.I.P. Snorkel” over the site that holds the remains of 1,100 Navy sailors and Marines who died at Pearl Harbor.

item.title: Senator Bill Cassidy, Targeted by Trump, Fights for Political Future in Louisiana Primary
item.description: Senator Bill Cassidy, targeted by President Trump, is walking a political tightrope as he battles other Republicans for the chance to seek a third term.
item.dc:creator: Michael Gold and Emily Cochrane
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Bill Cassidy was one of seven G.O.P. senators to vote to convict President Trump of inciting an insurrection after the Jan. 6 riot at the Capitol.

item.title: Back From China, Trump Faces Decision on Whether to Resume Strikes on Iran
item.description: Top aides have drafted battle plans as peace negotiations have stalled.
item.dc:creator: Helene Cooper, Eric Schmitt and Tyler Pager
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump has not yet decided whether to resume military strikes on Iran, but Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth told lawmakers that the Pentagon had “a plan to escalate if necessary.”

item.title: Republicans Waited to Challenge Trump on the Iran War. Now It May Be Too Late.
item.description: Having deferred to the president for months, G.O.P. lawmakers missed crucial milestones to try to limit his war powers. That has tied their hands in seeking parameters and exit criteria.
item.dc:creator: Megan Mineiro
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Lisa Murkowski, Republican of Alaska, during a Senate appropriation hearing last month.

item.title: Justice Dept. Aims to Use Terrorism Laws to Target Mexican Officials
item.description: In a new tactic, the Justice Department this week instructed federal prosecutors to build criminal drug cases against Mexican officials using terrorism statutes.
item.dc:creator: Ernesto Londoño, Alan Feuer and Jack Nicas
item.media:credit: Alejandro Cegarra for The New York Times
item.media:description: Flags at the U.S. border with Mexico in Tijuana last year.

item.title: Some Medicare Patients Can Now Get Free CBD
item.description: The Trump administration has authorized a test program to see if the cannabis compound can ease some symptoms and reduce health care costs among older patients.
item.dc:creator: Jan Hoffman
item.category: Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services
item.media:credit: Jackie Russo for The New York Times
item.media:description: CBD products are already popular with some Medicare-age patients.

item.title: Spotlight on China Adds to Some Chinese Americans’ Fears
item.description: The Trump-Xi summit emphasized stability. But China’s recruitment of foreign agents has fueled suspicion of Chinese Americans.
item.dc:creator: Amy Qin
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump and China’s leader, Xi Jinping, on Thursday in Beijing. Two criminal cases in the United States reflect the countries’ competitive and often tense geopolitical relationship.

item.title: In Kentucky, the Iran War Complicates a Republican Primary
item.description: Representative Thomas Massie, the incumbent, has opposed President Trump’s military strikes on Iran. He is now facing the biggest challenge of his career.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Medina and Michael Swensen
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Michael Swensen for The New York Times

item.title: Despite Tensions, China Greets Trump With Pomp and Pageantry
item.description: There was no major breakthrough in Beijing, but China pulled out all the stops for President Trump, with extravagant ceremonies and a state banquet.
item.dc:creator: John Yoon
item.category: Great Hall of the People (Beijing, China)
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Rows of cheering children welcomed President Trump at the Great Hall of the People in Beijing on Thursday.

item.title: Trump Touts ‘Fantastic Trade Deals’ With China, but Details Are Scarce
item.description: The president left Beijing following a summit with China, during which the two countries sought to stabilize their economic and political relations.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump before departing China in Beijing on Friday.

item.title: Medicare Coverage for GLP-1 Weight Loss Drugs: Cost, Eligibility and What to Know
item.description: What you should know about the federal government’s pilot program offering GLP-1s solely for weight loss.
item.dc:creator: Mark Miller
item.media:credit: Hannah Beier/Reuters
item.media:description: The list of GLP-1s that can be prescribed for obesity includes Eli Lilly’s Foundayo, Wegovy in injectable and tablet forms, and Eli Lilly’s Zepbound KwikPen.

item.title: A Republican’s Mysterious Absence Reflects Congress’s Silence on Health
item.description: Presidents are expected to tell the public basic health information, but members of the House and Senate often stay silent about medical conditions, even those that affect their ability to do their jobs.
item.dc:creator: Annie Karni
item.media:credit: Bryan Anselm for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Thomas H. Kean Jr., Republican of New Jersey, has not been seen on Capitol Hill or in his district for two months.

item.title: Bernie Sanders Endorses Slate of Candidates in Bid to Cement Progressive Legacy
item.description: The five dozen endorsements mostly feature those seeking state or local office who have previously supported the Vermont senator.
item.dc:creator: Shane Goldmacher
item.media:credit: Sarah Rice/Getty Images
item.media:description: Senator Bernie Sanders, an independent from Vermont, said he is working to help elect as many like-minded allies as possible up and down the ballot.

item.title: As Powell Steps Down, the Fed Confronts ‘Regime Change’
item.description: Jerome H. Powell is passing the chair’s baton to Kevin M. Warsh at the Federal Reserve, an institution that President Trump’s pick says needs an overhaul.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times, left; Haiyun Jiang for The New York Times

item.title: Oysterman, Veteran, Prep-School Alum: A Senate Candidate’s Complex Class Story
item.description: Democrats are pinning their hopes of flipping the Senate on Graham Platner, who has made his working-class persona key to his campaign. His background defies easy categorization.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Lerer, Katie Glueck and Sophie Park
